session.user:
Check and update the following documentation files.
Context: `PlanRef.plan_id` → `PlanRef.pr_id`. JSON `"plan_number"` → `"pr_number"`. Branch `planned-pr-context/{plan_id}` → `planned-pr-context/{pr_number}`. BUT `state.plan_id`, `PlanContext.plan_id`, TUI `plan_id` are UNCHANGED.
**Files to check:**
1. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/erk/branch-naming.md` — `plan_id` in plan-ref.json context → `pr_id` 2. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/erk/issue-pr-linkage-storage.md` — JSON `"plan_id": "123"` if it's plan-ref.json → `"pr_id": "123"` 3. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/erk/dispatch-ref-config.md` — `_detect_plan_number_from_context` function name (still in source?) and `resolve_plan_id_for_branch()` (still in source). Likely no changes. 4. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/erk/pr-address-workflows.md` — `PLAN-REVIEW-{plan_number}.md` file name pattern — check if this was renamed in source 5. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/erk/graphite-divergence-detection.md` — Check for relevant references 6. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/conventions.md` — Has `plan_id` in naming conventions table. Since PlanRef now uses `pr_id`, update the example. 7. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/integrations/linear-erk-mapping.md` — `plan_identifier` is a Linear concept, likely no change 8. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/testing/cli-testing.md` — `output["plan_id"]` — check context 9. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/testing/fake-api-migration-pattern.md` — `simulated_plan_number` — check if renamed 10. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/testing/import-conflict-resolution.md` — `parse_plan_number` function — check if still in source 11. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/testing/dual-backend-testing.md` — `plan.plan_identifier` — check if renamed 12. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/architecture/github-parsing.md` — `parse_plan_number_from_url` — check if renamed 13. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/architecture/agent-backpressure-gates.md` — `validate_plan_title` — check if renamed 14. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/pr-operations/plan-implementation-auto-force.md` — `state.plan_id` — leave as-is 15. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/planning/backend-naming-conventions.md` — Reference to plan-id-semantics doc 16. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/planning/planned-pr-branch-teleport.md` — `plan_number` in `github.get_pr(repo_root, plan_number)` — context dependent 17. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/planning/index.md` — References to plan_id in descriptions
Read each file, determine if changes are needed, then apply only correct edits. Skip files needing no changes.

1→--- 2→title: Branch Naming Conventions 3→read_when: 4→ - "creating or modifying branch name generation" 5→ - "extracting objective numbers from branch names" 6→ - "working with generate_planned_pr_branch_name(), or extract functions" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "constructing branch names manually" 9→ warning: "Use generate_planned_pr_branch_name() for consistent objective ID encoding." 10→ - action: "trying to extract plan number from branch name" 11→ warning: "Plan numbers are NOT encoded in branch names. Use plan-ref.json as the sole source of truth." 12→last_audited: "2026-02-24 00:00 PT" 13→audit_result: clean 14→--- 15→ 16→# Branch Naming Conventions 17→ 18→All erk-managed plan branches use the `plnd/` prefix. Plan numbers are **not** encoded in branch names — `.erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json` is the sole source of truth for plan-to-branch mapping. 19→ 20→## Canonical Function 21→ 22→<!-- Source: packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/naming.py, generate_planned_pr_branch_name --> 23→ 24→See `generate_planned_pr_branch_name()` in `packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/naming.py`. Accepts `title`, `timestamp`, and optional `objective_id` keyword argument. 25→ 26→## Branch Format 27→ 28→**Without objective:** 29→ 30→```

1→--- 2→title: Plan-PR Linkage Storage Model 3→read_when: 4→ - "understanding how plans link to PRs" 5→ - "debugging why a PR isn't linked to its plan" 6→ - "working with .erk/impl-context/plan-ref.json or .erk/impl-context/ref.json" 7→ - "creating PRs that close plans" 8→last_audited: "2026-02-17 00:00 PT" 9→audit_result: clean 10→--- 11→ 12→# Plan-PR Linkage Storage Model 13→ 14→This document describes how erk creates and stores the relationship between plans and their implementation pull requests. 15→ 16→## Overview 17→ 18→When a plan becomes a PR, erk establishes a linkage that allows: 19→ 20→- `erk pr list` to show which plans have associated PRs 21→- PRs to automatically close their source plan when merged 22→- The 🔗 indicator to appear for auto-closing PRs 23→ 24→## How Linkages Are Created 25→ 26→### Via `erk pr submit` 27→ 28→The primary path for creating plan-PR linkages: 29→ 30→1. Creates branch from plan (e.g., `P123-feature-12-11-0948`)
... [135 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: dispatch_ref Configuration 3→read_when: 4→ - "configuring which branch workflow_dispatch targets" 5→ - "working with .erk/config.toml dispatch settings" 6→ - "debugging workflow dispatch targeting the wrong branch" 7→ - "using --ref CLI option to override dispatch branch per run" 8→ - "using --ref-current to dispatch against current branch" 9→tripwires: 10→ - action: "assuming dispatch_ref is project-level config" 11→ warning: "dispatch_ref is repo-level config (.erk/config.toml), overridable at local level (.erk/config.local.toml). It is not project-level." 12→ - action: "passing both --ref and --ref-current to a dispatch command" 13→ warning: "--ref and --ref-current are mutually exclusive. resolve_dispatch_ref() raises UsageError if both are provided." 14→--- 15→ 16→# dispatch_ref Configuration 17→ 18→Override the default branch used for GitHub Actions `workflow_dispatch` events. By default, erk dispatches workflows against the repository's default branch (usually `master`). The `dispatch_ref` config allows targeting a different branch. 19→ 20→## Configuration 21→ 22→Top-level key in `.erk/config.toml`: 23→ 24→```toml 25→dispatch_ref = "my-custom-branch" 26→``` 27→ 28→Can be overridden per-user in `.erk/config.local.toml`: 29→ 30→```toml
... [89 lines omitted] ...
75→**Mutual exclusivity:** `--ref` and `--ref-current` cannot be used together. `resolve_dispatch_ref()` raises `UsageError` if both are provided. 77→**Detached HEAD:** Using `--ref-current` in a detached HEAD state raises a `UsageError` with a descriptive message, since there is no current branch to resolve.

1→--- 2→title: PR Address Workflows 3→read_when: 4→ - "addressing PR review comments" 5→ - "choosing between local and remote PR addressing" 6→ - "understanding erk launch pr-address" 7→ - "understanding /erk:pr-address command" 8→tripwires: 9→ - action: "resolving a review thread when the comment is a discussion comment (not a review thread)" 10→ warning: "Review threads and discussion comments use different GitHub APIs. resolve-review-threads only handles review threads. Discussion comments are resolved differently (or not at all)." 11→last_audited: "2026-03-05 00:00 PT" 12→audit_result: clean 13→--- 14→ 15→# PR Address Workflows 16→ 17→Erk provides two workflows for addressing PR review comments using Claude: 18→ 19→1. **Local** (`/erk:pr-address`) - Claude Code slash command, runs in your terminal 20→2. **Remote** (`erk launch pr-address --pr <number>`) - GitHub Actions workflow, runs in CI 21→ 22→## Decision Matrix 23→ 24→| Factor | Local | Remote | 25→| ---------------------------- | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------ | 26→| **Branch checkout** | Required (must be on branch) | Not required | 27→| **Interactive confirmation** | Yes (via Claude Code) | No | 28→| **Error recovery** | Immediate (fix and retry) | Check workflow logs | 29→| **Plan metadata tracking** | Manual | Automatic for `plnd/` branches | 30→| **Best for** | Active development | Queued/async work |
... [199 lines omitted] ...
180→- Job status (success/failure)

1→--- 2→title: Graphite Divergence Detection 3→read_when: 4→ - "debugging remote divergence errors during erk pr submit" 5→ - "understanding the Graphite-first submit flow's pre-checks" 6→ - "resolving 'branch is behind remote' errors" 7→ - "using --force flag with erk pr submit" 8→tripwires: 9→ - action: "running gt commands without --no-interactive" 10→ warning: "All gt commands MUST use --no-interactive. Without it, gt may prompt for input and hang indefinitely." 11→ - action: "pushing to a branch that may have been updated remotely without checking for divergence" 12→ warning: "The Graphite-first flow pre-checks for divergence before gt submit. Check with branch_exists_on_remote -> fetch_branch -> is_branch_diverged_from_remote." 13→ - action: "running raw git commands (checkout, branch) without gt track on a Graphite-managed branch" 14→ warning: "Raw git commands (checkout, branch) without `gt track` cause Graphite's cache to diverge from actual branch state. Use BranchManager which handles Graphite tracking automatically, or call `gt track` after raw git operations." 15→ score: 7 16→--- 17→ 18→# Graphite Divergence Detection 19→ 20→The Graphite-first submit flow includes proactive divergence detection before running `gt submit`. This prevents cryptic failures when the remote branch has been updated by CI, another session, or a prior submission. 21→ 22→## Guard Chain 23→ 24→<!-- Source: src/erk/cli/commands/pr/submit_pipeline.py:200-230 --> 25→ 26→The `_graphite_first_flow()` function in `submit_pipeline.py` runs a four-step guard chain before `gt submit`: 27→ 28→``` 29→branch_exists_on_remote() → fetch_branch() → is_branch_diverged_from_remote() → check behind > 0 30→```
... [48 lines omitted] ...
35→4. **Check `behind > 0`**: If the local branch is behind remote AND `effective_force` is `False`, return a `SubmitError` with `error_type="remote_diverged"`. Note: `effective_force = state.force or is_plan_impl`, so plan implementations (`state.plan_id is not None or state.branch_name.startswith("plnd/")`) auto-force and never hit this guard — it only applies to non-plan `erk pr submit` invocations. The `plnd/` branch prefix fallback handles cases where `.erk/impl-context/` was cleaned up before a failed push, leaving `plan_id` as `None`. 43→| On divergence | Rebase automatically | Error with resolution steps | 49→When divergence is detected, the error message suggests two options: 66→When `state.force` is `True` (from `erk pr submit -f`), the divergence check is bypassed entirely. The guard chain still runs `fetch_branch()` but skips the error return when `behind > 0`. This allows force-pushing over remote changes when the user explicitly opts in. 72→- [Draft PR Handling — Troubleshooting](../pr-operations/draft-pr-handling.md#troubleshooting-common-failures) - Related failure modes

1→--- 2→title: Code Conventions 3→read_when: 4→ - "naming functions or variables" 5→ - "creating CLI commands" 6→ - "naming Claude artifacts" 7→ - "moving code between packages" 8→ - "creating imports" 9→ - "creating immutable classes or frozen dataclasses" 10→ - "implementing an ABC with abstract properties" 11→tripwires: 12→ - action: "writing `__all__` to a Python file" 13→ warning: "Re-export modules are forbidden. Import directly from where code is defined." 14→ - action: "adding --force flag to a CLI command" 15→ warning: 'Always include -f as the short form. Pattern: @click.option("-f", "--force", ...)' 16→ - action: "adding a function with 5+ parameters" 17→ warning: "Load `dignified-python` skill first. Use keyword-only arguments (add `*` after first param). Exception: ABC/Protocol method signatures and Click command callbacks." 18→ - action: "editing source files directly on master branch" 19→ warning: "Never edit source files on master. Even for one-line fixes, use plan-first workflow. Bypasses review, CI gates, and worktree isolation." 20→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 21→audit_result: clean 22→--- 23→ 24→# Code Conventions 25→ 26→This document defines conventions that supplement AGENTS.md. For basic naming (snake_case, PascalCase, kebab-case), Claude artifacts naming, brand names, worktree terminology, and CLI command organization, see AGENTS.md directly. 27→ 28→## Variable Naming by Type 29→ 30→| Type | Convention | Example |
... [212 lines omitted] ...
145→class GitHubAPIFailed: 154→class GitHubAPIFailed: 220→## Pydantic Field() Exception 222→Pydantic `Field()` with defaults is an exception to erk's "no default parameters" rule. This applies only to Pydantic config/settings classes (e.g., `BaseSettings` subclasses), NOT to regular functions or dataclasses. 224→## Single-Use Locals Line-Length Exception

1→--- 2→title: Erk to Linear Concept Mapping 3→description: How erk concepts map to Linear's ontology 4→read_when: 5→ - Evaluating Linear as issue tracker for erk 6→ - Building Linear gateway 7→ - Understanding trade-offs between GitHub Issues and Linear 8→last_audited: "2026-02-05 20:38 PT" 9→audit_result: edited 10→--- 11→ 12→# Erk to Linear Concept Mapping 13→ 14→This document maps erk's current concepts to Linear's ontology, highlighting where Linear provides better primitives and where concepts don't map. 15→ 16→## Key Insight: AgentSession > Metadata Blocks 17→ 18→Linear's `AgentSession` is a **better fit** than erk's current GitHub metadata blocks for implementation tracking. 19→ 20→### Current Erk Approach 21→ 22→Erk embeds YAML in GitHub issue bodies via `plan-header` metadata blocks (see `packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/github/metadata/plan_header.py` and `schemas.py` for the full schema with 34+ fields). 23→ 24→Problems: 25→ 26→- No UI support (just raw YAML in collapsible) 27→- Manual parsing required 28→- No real-time updates 29→- No structured activity tracking 30→
... [191 lines omitted] ...
46→2. **Structured activities** - Thoughts, actions, errors are first-class types 152→ - type: "error" -> Failures 198→2. **Typed activities** - Thoughts, actions, errors rendered automatically

1→--- 2→title: CLI Testing Patterns 3→last_audited: "2026-02-16 00:00 PT" 4→audit_result: clean 5→read_when: 6→ - "writing tests for erk CLI commands" 7→ - "using ErkContext.for_test()" 8→ - "testing Click commands with context" 9→tripwires: 10→ - action: "testing code that reads ERK_PLAN_BACKEND or other environment variables via CliRunner" 11→ warning: "CliRunner env var isolation: ambient env vars from the developer shell leak into CliRunner by default and cause intermittent test failures. Use CliRunner(env={'ERK_PLAN_BACKEND': '...'}) to override, or CliRunner(env={}) to isolate completely. Never rely on ambient env being clean. Note: mix_stderr parameter is broken in Click 8.3.1 — do not use it." 12→ score: 7 13→ - action: "renaming a user-facing string in CLI output and updating related test assertions" 14→ warning: "Test assertion lag: after renaming display strings (e.g., 'issue' → 'plan'), grep all test files for the old literal before committing. Tests using old string literals against stale snapshots will silently pass — the failure only surfaces in CI on a clean checkout." 15→ score: 6 16→ - action: "passing mix_stderr parameter to CliRunner" 17→ warning: "Click 8.3.1 CliRunner no longer accepts mix_stderr parameter. Use CliRunner() or CliRunner(env={}) instead." 18→--- 19→ 20→# CLI Testing Patterns 21→ 22→This document covers testing patterns specific to erk CLI commands that use `ErkContext` for dependency injection. 23→ 24→## Overview 25→ 26→Erk CLI commands use Click's context system (`@click.pass_context`) to receive dependencies. Testing these commands requires passing a test context via the `obj` parameter to `CliRunner.invoke()`. 27→ 28→## The ErkContext.for_test() Pattern 29→ 30→`ErkContext.for_test()` is the canonical factory method for creating test contexts with fake dependencies.
... [491 lines omitted] ...
148→### Pattern 4: Testing Error Handling 150→When testing error paths: 166→ # Assert: Command exits with error 356→- [ ] Test both success and error paths

1→--- 2→title: FakePromptExecutor API Migration - Gateway to Core 3→last_audited: "2026-02-08 00:00 PT" 4→audit_result: edited 5→tripwires: 6→ - action: "using output=, should_fail=, or transient_failures= parameters in FakePromptExecutor" 7→ warning: "These are the deleted gateway API. Use simulated_* parameters (tests/fakes/) or prompt_results/streaming_events (erk_shared/core/fakes.py). See migration table." 8→ - action: "importing FakePromptExecutor from erk_shared.fakes.prompt_executor" 9→ warning: "This module was deleted in the consolidation. Import from tests.fakes.tests.prompt_executor instead." 10→read_when: 11→ - "writing tests that use FakePromptExecutor" 12→ - "choosing between the two FakePromptExecutor implementations" 13→ - "encountering old-style output=/should_fail= patterns in test code" 14→--- 15→ 16→# FakePromptExecutor API Migration: Gateway to Core 17→ 18→## Why This Migration Happened 19→ 20→PR #6587 consolidated two separate PromptExecutor abstractions into one: 21→ 22→1. **Gateway PromptExecutor** — single-shot prompts with simple `output`/`should_fail` constructor 23→2. **Core PromptExecutor** — full-featured with interactive, streaming, command, and prompt modes 24→ 25→The gateway version was deleted because it was a subset of the core's capabilities. The core ABC needed to support metadata extraction (PR URLs, issue numbers), event streaming (tool events, spinner updates), and hook interaction (zero-turns detection) — none of which the gateway's simple API could express. 26→ 27→## Two Current FakePromptExecutor Implementations 28→ 29→After the consolidation, two distinct fakes exist, serving different use cases: 30→
... [53 lines omitted] ...
33→The `tests/fakes/tests/` version is **scenario-based**: `simulated_*` params configure a single predetermined scenario. It returns same output every time and doesn't support multiple different responses. Tracks via internal lists with `.copy()` properties. Best for CLI command tests that need rich scenario simulation (PR creation, hook blocking, process errors). 44→| `should_fail=True` | `command_should_fail=True` | For command execution failures | 45→| `error="message"` | `simulated_prompt_error="message"` | For prompt execution failures | 46→| `transient_failures=2` | No equivalent | Retry logic moved to `RealPromptExecutor` in gateway layer | 54→- Process errors: `simulated_process_error` 60→| Simulate a specific execution scenario (PR creation, hook block, process error) | `tests/fakes/tests/prompt_executor.FakePromptExecutor` | 63→## Anti-Pattern: Failure Mode Confusion 65→The old gateway API had one failure flag (`should_fail`) for everything. The current API distinguishes between failure modes because they have different downstream effects: 67→- **`command_should_fail`** — the command itself fails (returns `CommandResult` with `success=False` or yields `ErrorEvent`) 68→- **`simulated_prompt_error`** — the prompt returns a `PromptResult` with `success=False` and an error message 69→- **`simulated_process_error`** — the subprocess can't start (yields `ProcessErrorEvent`) 73→Using the wrong failure mode produces the wrong event type, which means tests that check for specific error handling paths will pass for the wrong reason.

1→--- 2→title: Merge Conflict Resolution for Import Consolidation 3→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:30 PT" 4→audit_result: clean 5→read_when: 6→ - "resolving merge conflicts during rebase" 7→ - "fixing import conflicts after consolidation" 8→ - "rebasing after shared module changes" 9→--- 10→ 11→# Merge Conflict Resolution for Import Consolidation 12→ 13→When import paths are consolidated into shared modules, rebasing other branches can cause merge conflicts. This guide covers the resolution strategy. 14→ 15→## Common Scenario 16→ 17→You consolidated imports in one branch: 18→ 19→```python 20→# Before: multiple local helpers 21→from .local_helpers import parse_url 22→ 23→# After: shared module 24→from erk_shared.gateway.github.parsing import parse_url 25→``` 26→ 27→When rebasing other branches that still use the old imports, conflicts appear. 28→ 29→## Resolution Strategy 30→
... [105 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Plan Storage Testing 3→read_when: 4→ - "writing tests that involve plan storage" 5→ - "testing plan-related features" 6→ - "creating test helpers for plan store operations" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "writing plan storage tests that parametrize across both backends" 9→ warning: "After PR #8210, only the PlannedPRBackend exists. The GitHubPlanStore class was deleted. New plan-related tests should use PlannedPRBackend directly." 10→ - action: "using isinstance() to detect plan backend type in application code" 11→ warning: "Use plan_backend.get_provider_name() for backend-conditional logic (returns 'github-draft-pr'). isinstance checks couple to implementation classes. The provider name string is the stable API." 12→ score: 7 13→--- 14→ 15→# Plan Storage Testing 16→ 17→> **Note:** After PR #8210, the `GitHubPlanStore` class and `PlanStore` ABC were deleted. Only `PlannedPRBackend` exists. This document describes the test infrastructure for the current single-backend architecture. 18→ 19→## Test Helpers 20→ 21→All plan test helpers are in `tests/test_utils/plan_helpers.py`. 22→ 23→### `create_plan_store_with_plans()` 24→ 25→Creates a `PlannedPRBackend` pre-populated with plans, backed by `FakeGitHub`. 26→ 27→<!-- Source: tests/test_utils/plan_helpers.py, create_plan_store_with_plans --> 28→ 29→See `create_plan_store_with_plans()` in `tests/test_utils/plan_helpers.py` for the full signature. Returns `tuple[PlannedPRBackend, FakeGitHub]` — the backend and its backing fake for assertions. 30→

1→--- 2→title: GitHub URL Parsing Architecture 3→read_when: 4→ - "parsing GitHub URLs" 5→ - "extracting PR or issue numbers from URLs" 6→ - "understanding github parsing layers" 7→last_audited: "2026-02-05 15:05 PT" 8→audit_result: clean 9→--- 10→ 11→# GitHub URL Parsing Architecture 12→ 13→This document describes the two-layer architecture for parsing GitHub URLs and extracting identifiers (PR numbers, issue numbers, repo names). 14→ 15→## Architecture Overview 16→ 17→``` 18→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐ 19→│ CLI Layer │ 20→│ erk.cli.github_parsing │ 21→│ - Raises SystemExit on failure │ 22→│ - User-friendly error messages │ 23→│ - CLI-specific formatting │ 24→└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘ 25→ │ 26→ ▼ 27→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐ 28→│ Pure Parsing Layer │ 29→│ erk_shared.gateway.github.parsing │ 30→│ - Returns int | None │
... [77 lines omitted] ...
54→- Provides user-friendly error messages 82→Use pure functions for predictable testing (no exceptions to catch).

1→--- 2→title: Agent Back Pressure via Gates 3→read_when: 4→ - "designing validation for agent-generated output" 5→ - "choosing between silent transformation and rejection of invalid input" 6→ - "implementing retry loops for agent-produced values" 7→ - "adding validation to agent-facing APIs" 8→tripwires: 9→ - action: "silently transforming agent output (sanitize/normalize) instead of rejecting invalid values" 10→ warning: "Silent transformation masks mistakes and prevents the agent from learning. Use a validation gate that rejects invalid input with actionable feedback so the agent can self-correct." 11→ - action: "adding a validation gate without actionable feedback in the error message" 12→ warning: "Gates must include the expected pattern, the actual value, and examples so the agent can self-correct. See InvalidObjectiveSlug.message for the pattern." 13→ - action: "adding guidance to the agent without a programmatic gate to enforce it" 14→ warning: "Guidance without enforcement is optional compliance. The gate is the hard boundary. The agent should have guidance to help it succeed on the first try, but the gate is what enforces correctness." 15→last_audited: "2026-02-22 23:15 PT" 16→--- 17→ 18→# Agent Back Pressure via Gates 19→ 20→## Core Concept 21→ 22→A **gate** is any programmatic invariant check that an agent's output must pass through. The agent has maximum flexibility in _how_ it produces its output, but the gate enforces _what_ the output must look like. If the agent's output fails the gate, the workflow loops: the agent receives the failure feedback and retries until it passes. 23→ 24→## The Pattern is General 25→ 26→A gate can be: 27→ 28→- A regex check on a generated slug (simple) 29→- A type checker run on generated code (complex) 30→- A test suite that must pass (sophisticated)
... [48 lines omitted] ...
38→2. **Gate** — The programmatic check that enforces the invariant. This is the hard boundary. The agent cannot bypass it. The gate produces actionable feedback on failure so the agent can self-correct. 47→| Type checker (ty) | Type correctness | Type errors with locations | 48→| Test suite (pytest) | Behavioral correctness | Test failures with assertions | 59→- **Gates without actionable feedback**: The agent can't self-correct if the error message just says "invalid". Include the pattern, the actual value, and examples.

1→--- 2→title: Plan Implementation Auto-Force Push 3→read_when: 4→ - "debugging why erk pr submit force-pushed when --force was not specified" 5→ - "understanding force-push behavior for plan implementation branches" 6→ - "working with the submit pipeline for plan branches" 7→--- 8→ 9→# Plan Implementation Auto-Force Push 10→ 11→`erk pr submit` automatically applies force-push when the current branch is a plan implementation branch. This is expected behavior, not a bug. 12→ 13→## Detection 14→ 15→Auto-force is triggered when `state.plan_id is not None`, which is set when `.erk/impl-context/` is valid and contains issue tracking metadata. 16→ 17→<!-- Source: src/erk/cli/commands/pr/submit_pipeline.py, _run_phase1_graphite_submit --> 18→ 19→See `_run_phase1_graphite_submit()` in `src/erk/cli/commands/pr/submit_pipeline.py`. 20→ 21→## Why It's Safe 22→ 23→Plan implementation branches always diverge from remote because `erk implement` creates them fresh from trunk and commits `.erk/impl-context/` locally. Force-push is expected and harmless — there is no remote history worth preserving on a fresh implementation branch. 24→ 25→## User Experience 26→ 27→When auto-force triggers, the CLI prints: 28→ 29→``` 30→ Auto-forcing: plan implementation branch
... [17 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Backend Naming Conventions 3→read_when: 4→ - "referencing the plan backend by name in code or documentation" 5→ - "confused about draft_pr vs planned_pr vs github-draft-pr naming" 6→ - "adding user-facing output that mentions the backend" 7→--- 8→ 9→# Backend Naming Conventions 10→ 11→The plan backend has inconsistent naming across different contexts. This glossary documents the official name for each context. 12→ 13→## Naming Map 14→ 15→| Context | Name | Example | 16→| ------------------------- | ------------------ | -------------------------------------------------- | 17→| Provider name (API) | `github-draft-pr` | `plan_backend.get_provider_name()` returns this | 18→| Python class | `PlannedPRBackend` | `from erk_shared.plan_store.planned_pr import ...` | 19→| Config value (legacy) | `planned_pr` | Formerly `ERK_PLAN_BACKEND=planned_pr` | 20→| User-facing documentation | "planned PR" | "Plans are stored as planned pull requests" | 21→ 22→## Key Distinctions 23→ 24→- **`planned_pr`** (underscore) was the env var / config value 25→- **`PlannedPRBackend`** (no underscore, PascalCase) is the class name 26→- **`github-draft-pr`** (hyphenated) is the provider name string returned by the API 27→- **`draft_pr`** appears in some older references but is not an official name 28→ 29→## Which Name to Use 30→
... [18 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Planned PR Branch Teleport 3→read_when: 4→ - "implementing or debugging planned-PR plan setup" 5→ - "understanding branch teleport during plan implementation" 6→ - "working with setup_impl_from_pr for planned PR plans" 7→ - "debugging divergence between local and remote plan branches" 8→tripwires: 9→ - action: "implementing planned-PR plan without teleporting from remote" 10→ warning: "Before implementing a planned-PR plan, always teleport from remote: fetch_branch -> checkout/create_tracking -> pull_rebase" 11→ - action: "detecting plan backend by checking backend type directly" 12→ warning: "Use github.get_pr() + pr_result.head_ref_name to discover the plan branch. There is only one backend (planned-PR)." 13→ - action: "creating a new branch for a planned-PR plan" 14→ warning: "Planned PR plans already have a branch created during plan-save. Reuse the existing branch, don't create a new one." 15→ - action: "committing to planned-PR plan branches after checkout without pulling remote" 16→ warning: "Both setup_impl_from_pr.py and submit.py use the same three-step teleport: fetch_branch -> checkout/create_tracking -> pull_rebase. Skipping pull_rebase causes non-fast-forward push failures." 17→--- 18→ 19→# Planned PR Branch Teleport 20→ 21→When implementing a planned-PR plan, the local branch must be teleported from remote because plan-save creates the branch and pushes it, then checks out the original branch. Remote may receive additional commits before implementation runs. 22→ 23→## The Problem 24→ 25→1. `plan_save` creates a branch, pushes it to remote, creates draft PR 26→2. `plan_save` checks out the original branch (user continues working) 27→3. Time passes (remote CI may add commits, user may re-plan) 28→4. `plan-implement` runs and needs the latest branch state 29→ 30→## Three-Step Teleport Sequence
... [84 lines omitted] ...
87→PR #7697 added the missing `pull_rebase()` call to the submit path. Without it, the submit path would attempt to push commits onto a branch that had diverged from remote, causing non-fast-forward push failures.

1→<!-- AUTO-GENERATED FILE - DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY --> 2→<!-- Edit source frontmatter, then run 'erk docs sync' to regenerate. --> 3→ 4→# Planning Documentation 5→ 6→- **[agent-delegation.md](agent-delegation.md)** — delegating to agents from commands, implementing command-agent pattern, workflow orchestration 7→- **[agent-orchestration-safety.md](agent-orchestration-safety.md)** — passing data between agents via files or inline output, designing multi-agent workflows with parallel and sequential tiers, orchestrating subagents that produce markdown, XML, or other large outputs 8→- **[agent-orchestration.md](agent-orchestration.md)** — designing agent workflows with parallel and sequential tiers, choosing between parallel and sequential agent execution, adding or modifying agents in the learn pipeline, deciding which model tier to assign an agent 9→- **[agent-output-routing-strategies.md](agent-output-routing-strategies.md)** — deciding how agents should receive and produce outputs, designing multi-agent orchestration prompts, choosing between embedded-prompt and agent-file routing 10→- **[backend-naming-conventions.md](backend-naming-conventions.md)** — referencing the plan backend by name in code or documentation, confused about draft_pr vs planned_pr vs github-draft-pr naming, adding user-facing output that mentions the backend 11→- **[branch-name-inference.md](branch-name-inference.md)** — debugging missing branch_name in plans, implementing PR lookup from plans, modifying branch creation or naming conventions 12→- **[branch-plan-resolution.md](branch-plan-resolution.md)** — resolving which plan a branch belongs to, working with branch naming conventions for plans, understanding how get_plan_for_branch works 13→- **[complete-inventory-protocol.md](complete-inventory-protocol.md)** — estimating effort or remaining work for a plan or PR, closing a plan as complete, creating a consolidation plan from multiple PRs 14→- **[consolidation-labels.md](consolidation-labels.md)** — consolidating multiple learn plans, working with erk-consolidated label, preventing re-consolidation of issues, modifying /local:replan-learn-plans or /erk:replan consolidation behavior 15→- **[context-preservation-in-replan.md](context-preservation-in-replan.md)** — implementing or modifying replan workflow steps, debugging why a replanned issue produced a sparse plan, adding new plan-creation workflows that use EnterPlanMode 16→- **[context-preservation-prompting.md](context-preservation-prompting.md)** — writing slash commands that create plans, designing any workflow that calls EnterPlanMode, understanding why plans lose investigation context 17→- **[cornerstone-enforcement.md](cornerstone-enforcement.md)** — understanding SHOULD_BE_CODE filtering, working on learn pipeline classification, documentation items being classified as code, understanding cornerstone test rules 18→- **[cross-artifact-analysis.md](cross-artifact-analysis.md)** — detecting PR and plan relationships, assessing if work supersedes a plan, analyzing overlap between artifacts 19→- **[cross-repo-plans.md](cross-repo-plans.md)** — setting up plans in a separate repository, configuring [plans] repo in config.toml, understanding cross-repo plan closing syntax 20→- **[debugging-patterns.md](debugging-patterns.md)** — Debugging validation failures after an initial fix attempt fails, Encountering errors where the required format is unclear from the error message alone, Deciding whether to guess at another fix or read the validator source 21→- **[exploration-strategies.md](exploration-strategies.md)** — deciding when to use Explore agents vs direct searches, planning a two-stage explore-then-plan workflow, gathering codebase context before entering plan mode 22→- **[gateway-consolidation-checklist.md](gateway-consolidation-checklist.md)** — moving a gateway package into the gateway/ directory, extracting a new gateway from existing code, performing large-scale import refactoring across packages 23→- **[impl-context.md](impl-context.md)** — working with .erk/impl-context/ files, debugging plan content missing from a draft-PR implementation, reviewing a PR that contains .erk/impl-context/ files, understanding how draft-PR plans transfer content to .erk/impl-context/ 24→- **[incremental-dispatch.md](incremental-dispatch.md)** — dispatching implementation against an existing PR, adding a local plan to an existing PR for remote implementation, working with incremental-dispatch exec script or slash command 25→- **[label-scheme.md](label-scheme.md)** — understanding how plans and learn issues are labeled, working with erk-pr or erk-learn labels, debugging label filtering in TUI or CLI views 26→- **[learn-pipeline-workflow.md](learn-pipeline-workflow.md)** — debugging why learn materials are missing or malformed, understanding data flow from sessions to documentation plan, choosing between local learn and async learn modes, adding a new stage to the learn pipeline 27→- **[learn-plan-metadata-fields.md](learn-plan-metadata-fields.md)** — working with learn plan metadata, troubleshooting null learn_status or learn_plan_issue, transforming Plan objects in pipelines, understanding created_from_workflow_run_url field, adding workflow run backlinks to plans 28→- **[learn-plan-validation.md](learn-plan-validation.md)** — creating or modifying erk-learn plans, working on the learn workflow pipeline, debugging learn-on-learn cycle errors 29→- **[learn-session-embedding.md](learn-session-embedding.md)** — embedding session XML files in learn plan PRs, working with session preprocessing in the land or plan-save pipeline, debugging missing session XML in learn plan diffs 30→- **[learn-vs-implementation-plans.md](learn-vs-implementation-plans.md)** — choosing between plan types, creating erk-learn plans, understanding how learn plans relate to implementation plans, debugging learn plan base branch selection
... [53 lines omitted] ...
31→- **[learn-without-pr-context.md](learn-without-pr-context.md)** — debugging learn workflow failures where PR data is missing, implementing new learn pipeline steps that consume PR context, understanding why learn output lacks review feedback 35→- **[metadata-block-fallback.md](metadata-block-fallback.md)** — extracting plan content from GitHub issue comments, debugging 'no plan content found' errors in replan or plan-implement, working with older erk-plans that lack metadata blocks 39→- **[no-changes-handling.md](no-changes-handling.md)** — implementing erk-impl workflow, debugging no-changes scenarios, understanding erk-impl error handling 41→- **[one-shot-workflow.md](one-shot-workflow.md)** — working with erk one-shot dispatch, understanding how plans are autonomously created and implemented, debugging one-shot workflow failures, integrating objectives with one-shot dispatch 43→- **[plan-backend-migration.md](plan-backend-migration.md)** — migrating exec scripts from direct GitHubIssues to PlanBackend, implementing LBYL pattern for plan existence checks, handling PlanNotFound vs PlanHeaderNotFoundError 47→- **[plan-id-semantics.md](plan-id-semantics.md)** — calling github.get_pr() or github.get_issue() with a plan_id, writing code that handles plan IDs, debugging 404 errors when fetching plan metadata 50→- **[plan-schema.md](plan-schema.md)** — understanding plan structure, debugging plan validation errors, working with plan-header or plan-body blocks 60→- **[pr-submission-patterns.md](pr-submission-patterns.md)** — creating or updating PRs programmatically in erk, debugging why a duplicate PR or issue was created, fixing erk pr check validation failures, understanding the PR number vs plan number distinction 68→- **[session-preprocessing.md](session-preprocessing.md)** — modifying session preprocessing or compression pipeline, understanding why there are two preprocessing implementations, linking sessions to plans for learn workflow, debugging preprocessing failures or empty session output 69→- **[stacked-plan-branch-rebase.md](stacked-plan-branch-rebase.md)** — working with stacked plans that have non-trunk parents, debugging gt track failures for plan branches, understanding how plan branches integrate with Graphite stacking 70→- **[sub-agent-context-limitations.md](sub-agent-context-limitations.md)** — delegating session-dependent commands to Task tool sub-agents, debugging impl-signal or plan-save failures with empty session IDs, designing workflows that split work between root agent and sub-agents

Since branch names do not encode plan numbers, `plan-ref.json` ...[truncated 154 chars]...nd.get_plan_for_branch()`** resolves plans by looking up the PR associated with the branch
